model_narrative: This is a routing problem. A salesman travels among a set of cities, and for every ordered pair of cities there is a known cost of going directly from one to the other. We decide which direct legs between cities to include in the route. The objective is to make the total cost of the chosen legs as small as possible.

sets.name: ii
sets.doc: all candidate cities on the map. Only the cities listed in i belong to this tour, and every leg of the route runs from one tour city to another tour city; the remaining candidate cities carry no legs

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the cities that are actually on this tour; the salesman must visit each of them. The first city listed is the start of the tour and its tour position is fixed to 1

sets.name: j
sets.doc: an alias of the tour cities i, used to index the destination end of a leg

params.name: c
params.doc: travel cost of going directly from the first city to the second city

vars.name: x
vars.doc: 1 if the route goes directly from the first city to the second, 0 otherwise. A city cannot follow itself so the same-city legs are fixed to 0

vars.name: u
vars.domain: Reals in [1, number of tour cities]
vars.doc: the position of a city in the visiting order along the tour; the start city is fixed at position 1

vars.name: z
vars.doc: total travel cost of the route

constraints.name: z
constraints.intent: Define the route's total travel cost as the cost of every chosen leg added up over all pairs of tour cities, and set the objective value equal to that total.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each tour city must be left exactly once. For every city, the salesman departs from it to exactly one other city.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each tour city must be entered exactly once. For every city, the salesman arrives at it from exactly one other city.

constraints.name:
i)
first_city
constraints.intent: The chosen legs must form one single tour through all the cities rather than several smaller disconnected loops. Use the tour-position variables to order the cities so that whenever a leg from one city to another is used, the destination city comes later in the order than the origin city, which rules out any sub-loop that does not pass through the start city.

query: Roadworks are about to close the direct link between the last two cities on our roster in both directions for the season. With that connection unavailable, what does the best route cost?

query: There's a proposal on the table to make sure the route doesn't lean entirely on quick local hops — the loop should commit to at least two genuine long-haul legs, meaning legs whose distance is in the upper half of the distance range across all city pairs. Build that in and tell me what the resulting tour distance comes to.
expected_pyomo:
def _long_legs(model):
    costs = [value(model.c[i, j]) for i in model.i for j in model.i if i != j]
    threshold = 0.5 * max(costs)
    return sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.i for j in model.i if i != j and value(model.c[i, j]) >= threshold) >= 2
model.long_legs = Constraint(rule=_long_legs)

query: We're looking at an anti-clustering rule for dispatch: no stop should be allowed to depart straight to its single closest neighbour — each city has to leave for somewhere other than its nearest one. Apply that to every city and let me see what the tour distance becomes.

query: The route threads straight through the whole 1-2-3 cluster, chaining all three neighbours back to back. Wouldn't it be healthier to break that up and use at most one leg inside the 1-2-3 group? What's the model seeing that makes it want to chain them?
expected_pyomo:
def _spread(model):
    cluster = list(model.i)[:3]
    return sum(model.x[a, b] for a in cluster for b in cluster if a != b) <= 1
model.spread_123 = Constraint(rule=_spread)

query: The whole plan seems to be coasting on cheap little hops and ducking the longer legs wherever it can. That can't be a robust loop. Shouldn't a sensible tour use at least as many long-haul legs — the ones in the upper half of the distance range — as it does short hops? What's keeping the model from balancing it that way?
expected_pyomo:
def _long_over_short(model):
    costs = [value(model.c[i, j]) for i in model.i for j in model.i if i != j]
    threshold = 0.5 * max(costs)
    long_legs = sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.i for j in model.i if i != j and value(model.c[i, j]) >= threshold)
    short_legs = sum(model.x[i, j] for i in model.i for j in model.i if i != j and value(model.c[i, j]) < threshold)
    return long_legs >= short_legs
model.long_over_short = Constraint(rule=_long_over_short)

query: The plan keeps leaning on the direct shortcut between city 1 and city 3. That feels like a single point of failure — couldn't we serve the loop without ever using a direct 1-3 leg? What's the trade-off keeping it on that shortcut?
